textPlain: I have to admit that discovering that ProtonVPN was actually just owned by Proton Technologies feels underwhelming.
This link displays just the map, freed from it's painfully small frame.https://kumu.io/embed/9ced55e897e74fd807be51990b26b415#vpn-c...

Been saying it for YEARS: 95% of VPNs sell your data. It's where they make their money. It's absolutely insane the push-back I get when I say this online. I get downvoted to hell and back.Source: I bought this data from VPN companies... Hell, you can inject ads and surveys if you want!
Is there any other real world usecases for VPN nowadays other than:1. Getting access to geolocked data2. Torrenting "Linux ISOs"?
Just pay for and use Mullvad.

3. Hosting websites with DDNS (though the abuse from that caused Mullvad and IVPN to drop port forwarding)4. Though it hurts anonymity, and is relatively rare: I2P or Hyphanet, because some websites block known P2P nodes[1]. Important if your bank or work is being a jerk about it.5. As ThatMedicIsASpy notes, ISP issues: some routers soil the bed from P2P, some ISP's throttle P2P traffic regardless of legality, etc.[1] https://old.reddit.com/r/i2p/comments/tc3bhs/is_anybody_else...
Free wifi hotspotsNowadays most traffic is tls encrypted, but there are still metadata that can be collected.
Those two are pretty big already to be honest. I guess a third one would be avoiding eavesdropping on public wi-fis.
Just spin up a server with wireguard.
I do and I like them, but Cloudflare blocks their ips aggressively.
I did until they killed port forwarding.
